rationale: Anti-gaming lens: the gate must be correct BY ITSELF, with a falsifiable contract, not by emergent downstream luck. Only A does that. I verified on git 2.51.2 that `show-ref --exists` yields a genuine 0/2/1 (present/absent/error) split — the exit-2 absent branch means a cleanly-deleted (normal post-complete) bead never false-refuses, and error=1 fails the gate closed. That is directly pinnable by TestBranchExistsE_ThreeWay/UnreadableRefStore as an isolated, RED-on-revert AC. I stress-tested C's fact-5 and it HOLDS: a single unreadable ref makes `--quiet` exit 1 (masked absent) but `git merge bead/orphan` fails on the same corrupt ref; masked-probe implies merge-also-fails because merge needs strictly more ref-readability than the probe, and MergeBase@:249 catches whole-store faults pre-scan. I could construct no un-gated-merge path. BUT C's safety is EMERGENT and UNPINNED — no AC fails if that coupling breaks, and the spec itself says blp6 will later modify the very FinalizeEpic merge loop the refutation leans on. Anti-gaming distrusts prose refutations of unasserted invariants. A ends the rounds-2-5 spiral with a purpose-built primitive; its only cost is an explicit, CI-enforceable git>=2.43 floor. Critical: floor must be HARD (refuse fail-closed on older git with a version message) with NO --quiet classifier fallback — a fallback reopens the exact fail-open A exists to close.
rejected: C: refutation empirically holds, but gate correctness rests on emergent, AC-unpinned coupling (MergeBase@:249 + FinalizeEpic merge-failure) that blp6 is slated to disturb — safe today, silently regressible tomorrow. B: --quiet + store-health precheck only covers the whole-store case (already caught by :249); the single-ref residual still falls to --quiet masking and reduces to C's emergent safety, or a per-ref health probe reintroduces the exit-code spiral. D: worktree-enumeration diverges from the shared FindOrphanedClosedBeads predicate that complete/next/doctor use — largest blast radius for the least incremental gain over A.
